title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: California LCFS Market Saw an Increase in Type 1 Trade Volumes, Accompanied By a Slight Uptick in Prices
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(3rd February â€“ 9th February)
  • A total of 1,595,677 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a 4.43x times rise week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades witnessed a 2.19x times rise WoW as 2,378, 968 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price increased this week to $72.94/MTÂ for Type 1Â transfers, recording a 4.32%Â rise WoW; All Non-Zero transfers rose by 0.75% in price, reaching $71.23/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price increased by 3.23% WoW; on 11th Feb, the spot price stood at $73.13/MT and the benchmark price stood at $77.00/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices increased by 1.66% WoW, D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increased by 6.85% WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

The California LCFS market has experienced a positive trend in both trade volumes and prices over the past week. Type 1 prices saw a notable increase, with all non-zero credit prices also rising slightly. Trading volumes for Type 1 credits surged, reflecting a substantial uptick compared to the previous week, while overall transaction volume for all non-zero trades experienced a decrease. The Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS) credits also showed growth, with a rise in prices across various categories.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_592141" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 4.32% WoW rise, increasing from $69.92 to $72.94 this week, with all non-zero credit prices increased by 0.75% WoW, rising from $70.70 to $71.23. Type 1 trades saw 1,595,677 credits exchanged, reflecting a 4.43x times increase. The total transaction volume for all non-zero trades saw a 2.19x times decrease to reach 2,378,968 credits.

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_592142" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices increased by 3.23% WoW, reaching $73.50/MT. On 11th February, the average spot price was $73.13/MT, while the benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $77.00/MT on 11th February.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_592143" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 79 trades were reported in the market, with 15 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $97.50 for a Type 1 transaction at a volume of 6,000 credits. Last week, the lowest transfer by price was $46.00 per credit of Type 2, reported for a transaction at volume of 10,000 credits. The largest volume transfers of 500,000 credits of the Type 1 category were undertaken for a price of $73.50, whereas the smallest transfer volume was reported for a transaction of 223 credits of the Type 2 category at $74.00.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_592144" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] In January, the credit volume was 6.72 MMT, a slight decrease from December's 7.38 MMT. Meanwhile, the Type 1 average spot price saw a modest increase, rising from $71.89 in December 2024 to $72.56 in January 2025. Trading volumes showed notable year-over-year (YOY) growth, reflecting a 31.25% increase from the 5.12 MMT recorded in January of the previous year. By February 12, the Type 1 average spot prices had risen further, reaching $72.94.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_592145" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 3rd Feb â€“ 9th Feb, total VPT recorded a 3.97% increase (WoW), with all non-zero prices rising over the previous week.Â 

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices increased by 1.66% WoW, D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increased by 6.85% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

  • Internal Revenue Bulletin Publishes Notice on 45Z Proposed Regulations and Guidance on Emissions Rates. Notice 2025-10 published in the Internal Revenue Bulletin announces forthcoming proposed regulations on the clean fuel production credit under Â§ 45Z (Â§ 45Z credit), enacted under the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022. The notice explains the forthcoming proposed regulations and requests public comments on the draft text of the forthcoming proposed regulations. Notice 2025-11 provides taxpayers with guidance about emissions rates, including the initial emissions rate table,Â  for the clean fuel production credit.
  • Oregon House Committee Holds Hearing On Delaying Clean Trucks Rule. The Oregon House Committee on Climate, Energy and Environment held a public hearing on House Bill 3119 on Thursday. The bill is a bipartisan effort to delay Oregon's Advanced Clean Trucks rule, which went into affect Jan. 1. It would declare an emergency and demands the state's Department of Environmental Quality to delay adoption of the regulations until Jan. 1, 2027.
  • S. DOT Federal Highway Administration Issues Guidance Suspending Approval of State Electric Vehicle Infrastructure Deployment Plans. The new leadership of the Department of Transportation (U.S. DOT) has decided to review the policies underlying the implementation of the National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure (NEVI) Formula Program. Accordingly, the current NEVI Formula Program Guidance dated June 11, 2024, and all prior versions of this guidance are rescinded. The FHWA is updating the NEVI Formula Program Guidance to align with current U.S. DOT policy and priorities, including those set forth in DOT Order 2100.7, titled â€œEnsuring Reliance Upon Sound Economic Analysis in Department of Transportation Policies, Programs, and Activities.â€
  • "Let Green Gas Count": Global Call for GHG Protocol To Fully Recognise Robust Market Instruments in End-Usersâ€™ Scope 1 Emissions Inventories. A global consortium of organizations in the supply chain of renewable gaseous fuels and derivatives have called on the GHG Protocol to adopt a market-based approach for renewable gases in Scope 1 inventory and issue an interim statement in the first half of 2025 confirming that robust market instruments will be recognised in GHG inventories.
